coredata.dc:title: H-branching DNA nanostructures combined with dual-emitting carbon quantum dots for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Jianwei
Zhang, Jinmin
Liang, Ruirui
Shen, Hailin
Suo, Zhiguang
Jin, Huali
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metals pose a serious threat to human life and the ecological environment due to their low degradability and toxicity. Among them, lead ions (Pb2+) and cadmium ions (Cd2+) are easily enriched in food and drinking water of various plants and animals, and are difficult to be degraded, thus seriously jeopardizing human health. The development of sensitive, rapid, safe and reliable heavy metal detection methods is of great practical value for maintaining food safety. Here, cleverly used H-branched DNA nanostructures are formed by combining the target recognition elements Pb2+-DNAzyme and Cd2+-Apt with specific rolling circle amplification (RCA) products. In addition, two different carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared as signal probes, blue carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) and red carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs), with high fluorescence quantum yield and stable fluorescence performance. A novel layered nanomaterial, MXene-Ti3C2, was also prepared as a quenching material, characterized by large surface area and good fluorescence quenching performance. Therefore, a sensitive and efficient dual-target fluorescent biosensor was constructed by utilizing the photoinduced electron transfer effect between CQDs and Ti3C2. Under the optimal conditions, the sensor showed good linearity over the Pb2+ concentration range of 0.5–1000 nM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.11 nM. The sensor also showed good linearity over the Cd2+ concentration range of 0.5–500 nM and a LOD of 0.116 nM. Compared with other types of simultaneous detection methods for Pb2+ and Cd2+, this dual-target fluorescent sensor has excellent detection performance. Moreover, the sensor has good stability, repeatability and reproducibility, and has good application prospects in food safety detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent biosensor
Dual-emitting CQDs
Ti3C2
H-branched DNA nanostructures
Pb2+
Cd2+

Application of dual-target fluorescent biosensors for real samples detection
Characterization and properties of dual-emission fluorescent CQDs
Study of the properties of the nanomaterial MXene-Ti3C2
Principle of dual-target fluorescent biosensor for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+
Detection performance analysis of dual-target fluorescent biosensor
Performance study of dual-target fluorescent sensor
Dual-target fluorescent biosensor analysis of real samples

Thanks the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their invaluable help with XPS and XRD analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A) and histogram of particle size distribution (inset of Fig. 1A) for B-CQDs; TEM (B) and histogram of particle size distribution (inset of Fig. 1B) for R-CQDs; UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CQDs after binding to DNAzyme-1 (C) and R-CQDs after binding to Apt (D); UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of B-CQDs (E) and R-CQDs (F) at optimal excitation and emission wavelengths (inset: two CQDs under natural light and UV light).
SEM of Ti3C2 (A); Total elemental mapping of MXene-Ti3C2 (B) and individual elemental maps (C); X-ray energy spectrometry analysis of MXene-Ti3C2 (D).
(A)XRD of Ti3AlC2 and MXene-Ti3C2; XPS full survey spectrum of MXene-Ti3C2 (B) and high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p (C), F 1s (D), O 1s (E) and C 1s(F).

Fluorescence spectra of B-CQDs (A) and R-CQDs (B) at different quenching material: Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2 (24 h) and Ti3C2 (48 h); Fluorescence spectra of B-CQDs (C) and R-CQDs (D) at different concentrations of Ti3C2; Fluorescence decay curves of B-CQDs (E) and R-CQDs (F).
Diagram of the position (A) and specific magnification details (B) corresponding to RANK#1 on the aptamer.

Fluorescence spectra of B-CQDs after incubation of the prepared fluorescent sensors with different concentrations of Pb2+ (0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000 nM) (A); Fluorescence of the sensors at different concentrations of Pb2+ (0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000 nM) intensity difference ΔF change (B) (inset: linear relationship between ΔF value and logarithm of Pb2+ concentration); Fluorescence spectra of R-CQDs after incubation of prepared fluorescent sensors with different concentrations of Cd2+ (0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500 nM) (C); Fluorescence spectra of R-CQDs at different concentrations of Cd2+ (0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000 nM) the fluorescence intensity difference ΔF change of the sensor (D) (inset: linear relationship between ΔF value and logarithm of Cd2+ concentration).

Repeatability (A) and reproducibility (B) studies of the sensors; Stability studies of the sensors for the detection of Pb2+ (C) and Cd2+ (D) under different storage conditions; Specificity studies of the sensors (E); (Pb2+ and Cd2+ concentrations of 50 nM, and 50000 nM for other heavy metal ions).

Synthesis of B-CQDs and R-CQDs and preparation of H-type branches (A); Preparation of Ti3C2 (B); Formation of RCA DNA nanostructures (C); Schematic diagram of the detection of a dual-target fluorescent biosensor (D).
Determination of real samples using constructed fluorescent sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: H-branching DNA nanostructures combined with dual-emitting carbon quantum dots for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety Inspection and Control, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety Inspection and Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety Inspection and Control, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: H-branching DNA nanostructures combined with dual-emitting carbon quantum dots for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+.

Heavy metals pose a serious threat to human life and the ecological environment due to their low degradability and toxicity. Among them, lead ions (Pb2+) and cadmium ions (Cd2+) are easily enriched in food and drinking water of various plants and animals, and are difficult to be degraded, thus seriously jeopardizing human health. The development of sensitive, rapid, safe and reliable heavy metal detection methods is of great practical value for maintaining food safety. Here, cleverly used H-branched DNA nanostructures are formed by combining the target recognition elements Pb2+-DNAzyme and Cd2+-Apt with specific rolling circle amplification (RCA) products. In addition, two different carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared as signal probes, blue carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) and red carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs), with high fluorescence quantum yield and stable fluorescence performance. A novel layered nanomaterial, MXene-Ti3C2, was also prepared as a quenching material, characterized by large surface area and good fluorescence quenching performance. Therefore, a sensitive and efficient dual-target fluorescent biosensor was constructed by utilizing the photoinduced electron transfer effect between CQDs and Ti3C2. Under the optimal conditions, the sensor showed good linearity over the Pb2+ concentration range of 0.5–1000 nM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.11 nM. The sensor also showed good linearity over the Cd2+ concentration range of 0.5–500 nM and a LOD of 0.116 nM. Compared with other types of simultaneous detection methods for Pb2+ and Cd2+, this dual-target fluorescent sensor has excellent detection performance. Moreover, the sensor has good stability, repeatability and reproducibility, and has good application prospects in food safety detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization, the frequency of the use of heavy metals has increased, and the resulting heavy metal pollution has become one of the most serious worldwide pollution hazards [1]. They mainly originate from the irrational discharge of metal wastes in industrial production and the excessive use of sewage, pesticides and fertilizers in agricultural production [2,3]. It is found that a variety of heavy metal ions often coexist in aquatic environments, soil, food and drinking water. Among them, Pb2+ and Cd2+ were two typical pollutants produced by the chemical industry. They can irreversibly change the structure of proteins, causing them to lose their functions completely, posing a great risk to human health and the environment [4]. The research has shown that multiple metal ions caused more significant stacked toxicity and synergistic effects than the toxicity of a single metal ion [5]. This is because individual heavy metals at low concentrations can stimulate higher toxicity in the presence of other environmental toxicants in the same system [6]. Therefore, the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+ in food and the environment was particularly urgent.

To date, several methods have been applied to detect Pb2+ and Cd2+, including High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [7], Capillary Electrophoresis (CE) [8], Mass Spectrometry (MS) [9] and Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) [10]. However, the disadvantage of these methods was the complexity of the equipment and the need for specialized operators [11–13]. With the development of sensing technology and material science, various types of biosensors are becoming alternative methods for rapid detection of heavy metal ions contamination. In recent years, electrochemical biosensors have attracted much attention in the research field [14]. Electrochemical sensors, as a new type of detection means, have the disadvantages of insufficient sensitivity, poor stability and time-consuming at the same time. Among them, fluorescent sensors have received extensive attention from researchers in the field of detection due to their advantages of simple preparation, cost-effectiveness and fast response [15–17]. Based on these investigations, the use of fluorescent aptamer sensors was chosen because of their low cost and fast response time, which better meets the requirements of realizing rapid detection.

CQDs are a novel type of carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescence characteristics [18]. Compared with ordinary semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs have excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility and simple preparation methods. Thus, CQDs are considered to be an efficient fluorescent sensing material [19,20]. In addition, the CQDs have optical properties that make fluorescent sensors more sensitive. The presence of abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs makes it easy to couple with other biomolecules [21]. The preparation of fluorescent bioprobes by coupling CQDs with nucleotides can be flexibly applied to the construction of fluorescent biosensors. Wang et al. used a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) for the highly sensitive and selective quantitative detection of trace Hg2+ in tap water [22]. At the same time, CQDs have the disadvantage of a high background signal. It is often necessary to use quenching materials to reduce the background signal and thus increase the sensitivity of the sensor.

In recent years, two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets have shown promising applications in many domains such as sensors, optoelectronic devices, energy conversion, and storage due to their unique electrical, optical, and interfacial capabilities [23]. MXene-Ti3C2, as an emerging fluorescent quenching material, has an ideal large specific surface area, broad and strong absorption properties, superhydrophilicity and good biocompatibility. In addition, the surface of the sheet contains abundant functional group sites conducive to the modification of various biomolecules, which makes it an important energy acceptor for the preparation of fluorescent sensors [24]. Lin et al. achieved a highly sensitive detection of deoxynivalenol (DON) using single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) modified upconversion nanoparticles as fluorescent probes and MXene-Au as a quencher [25]. Therefore, considering the reason of high background signal, some materials with large surface area and good quenching effect can be used to reduce the background signal so as to realize highly sensitive detection.

However, the presence of interfering substances and chemicals in complex environmental matrices often interferes with the fluorescent signal response of the target, leading to possible errors in detection and quantification. To further improve detection selectivity, functional nucleic acids are often introduced into the construction of fluorescent sensors [26]. Aptamers (Apt) and DNAzyme are highly specific for almost any metal ion and can be obtained from large DNA libraries by in vitro screening [27,28]. Compared to antibodies, aptamers are more specific, stable and resistant to interference [29]. Hong et al. established a fast and cost-effective fluorescent sensor based on a Cd2+-specific aptamer, which can be further used to monitor the rapid leaching kinetics of heavy metal ions in vitro and in living cells in real time [30]. Therefore, the combination of functional nucleic acids with fluorescence sensing technology constitutes a more selective and sensitive fluorescent biosensor for the monitoring of heavy metal ions.

Rolling cyclic amplification (RCA) is a highly efficient isothermal method of nucleic acid amplification that produces highly elongated ssDNA with thousands of repetitive sequences [31]. RCA products are ideal scaffolds for providing output signaling probes, such as fluorescent molecular probes for conjugation [32]. In addition, DNA is a multifunctional biomaterial that can be skillfully designed and assembled into DNA nanostructures with good rigidity, stability, biocompatibility, and programmability to realize innovative biological functions. Usually, base complementary pairing of two or more DNA can form some special DNA nanostructures, such as Y-type [33], DNA tweezers [34], DNA tetrahedron [35] and other structures. In recent years, it has been found that DNA nanostructures with specific morphology can also be obtained by the roll-over amplification reaction for the preparation of biosensors. Therefore, a specific morphology can be used for fixing the signal probe and combining it with the RCA to improve sensor stability, response efficiency and signal amplification.

In summary, a sensitive and rapid novel homogeneous fluorescent biosensor was prepared for the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+ in food matrices. Functional nucleic acids were skillfully assembled into H-branched DNA nanostructures, which were then modified with two types of CQDs. These CQDs with different emission spectra can be used as target recognition units and fluorescent signal probes. Two different emitting CQDs fluorescent probes were successfully prepared with uniform size, stable fluorescence signal and high fluorescence quantum yield. The two-dimensional layered material Ti3C2 was used as a fluorescence quencher with a good quenching effect, resulting in a lower background signal for the sensor. The repeatability, reproducibility and specificity of the sensors were investigated under optimal conditions and the results were satisfactory. Surprisingly, the stability of CQDs can reach up to 31 days under light-avoidance conditions. In addition, spiked samples of tap water and pickle and quality control samples (rice flour, kelp and porcine liver powder) were examined, and the results demonstrated that the fluorescent biosensor has good practical applicability.

Nucleic acid sequences were synthesized, modified and purified by Shanghai Sangong Biotechnology Co. (Shanghai, China) for synthesis, modification and purification, and the specific base sequences were shown in the Supporting Information. Details of the instrumentation and materials used in this fluorescence sensor can be found in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the dual-target fluorescent biosensor for Pb2+ and Cd2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The RCA reaction product was first prepared. 1 μL 100 μM primer chain and 1 μL 100 μM circular template were placed in a 200 μL centrifuge tube. Subsequently, 20 μL 10 × T4 Buffer and 178 μL ultrapure water were added to it, and it was annealed at 95°C for 5 min in a water bath. After that, 5μL T4 ligase was added, and the reaction was carried out overnight at 16°C for 12 h in a PCR instrument, and was inactivated at 65°C for 10 min. Continue to add 3 μL Phi29 polymerase, 30 μL dNTPs, 30 μL 10 × Phi29 Buffer and 30 μL ultrapure water, and the reaction was carried out in a water bath at 30°C for 12 h. The RCA reaction was completed.

Then the fluorescence reaction system was prepared. 10 μL 1 μM B-CQDs/DNAzyme-1, 10 μL 1 μM B-CQDs/DNAzyme-2, 10 μL 1 μM S1, and 10 μL 2 μM R-CQDs/Apt were placed in centrifugal tubes, mixed thoroughly, and incubated with oscillation at 37°C for 2 h. After that, 2 μL RCA product was added, and the system was incubated with oscillation at 37°C for 1 h. Add 20 μL of blank solution and 20 μL of mixed solution containing Pb2+ and Cd2+ to the system at the same time. Subsequently, 2 μL the RCA product was added to the system and incubated with oscillation at 37°C for 1 h. Then 20 μL blank solution and 20 μL mixed solution containing Pb2+ and Cd2+ were added, and incubated at 37°C for 1 h. Finally, 10 μL 10 mg/mL Ti3C2 material was added, and incubated at 37°C for 40 min, and then buffer solution was replenished to 200 μL for fluorescence assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of dual-target fluorescent biosensors for real samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the analytical potential of the developed dual-target fluorescent sensor in real samples, pickle and tap water samples were spiked using standard addition recovery experiments. Tap water was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane. Solid sample were pre-treated by microwave digestion. 0.5 g of crushed solid sample, 2 mL of H2O2 and 8 mL of HNO3 were mixed in a digestion jar and placed in a microwave disintegrator. Sample were digested according to a pre-set program (180 °C reaction temperature, 400 psi pressure, 12 min heating time and 15 min hold time). After the digestion was completed, the digestion tank was removed and placed on an electric hot plate at 160°C to drive out the acid, after which it was cooled at room temperature. Ultrapure water was supplemented to 10 mL into the digestion solution, and the specific procedure was referred to previous studies. Finally, the spiked concentrations of Pb2+ and Cd2+ were 5, 110 and 215 nM, and all samples were detected using the optimized experimental procedure, which was described in Section 2.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of dual-emission fluorescent CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CQDs and R-CQDs, were first characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As can be seen from Fig. 1 A–B, the dual-emission fluorescent CQDs were almost spherical particles and uniformly dispersed. Statistical analysis of the particle size showed that the particle size of B-CQDs tended to be Gaussian, with a particle size distribution ranging from 4.5-10 nm and an average size of 6.95 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). The particle size distribution of R-CQDs ranged from 7.76-9.99 nm, with an average size of 8.76 nm (inset of Fig. 1B). In the fluorescent sensor, CQDs were labeled to the end of DNA by amide bonding and verified by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) absorption spectrograms. As can be seen in Fig. 1C, the B-CQDs have an absorption peak of their own at around 335 nm. After ligating with DNAzyme-1, a new absorption peak appeared at 260 nm, indicating that B-CQDs and DNAzyme-1 could be successfully ligated. From Fig. 1D, it can be seen that R-CQDs have two absorption peaks at 230 and 260 nm. After ligating with Apt, its absorption peak at 260 nm was blue shifted, which also indicated that B-CQDs and Apt could be successfully ligated. All of the above characterization results were consistent with the morphological features of CQDs [36], and it can be ligated to DNA for signal probe use.

The optical properties of two CQDs were investigated by Fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Fig. 1E, there was an obvious absorption peak at 332 nm, which corresponds to the n-π* leap of the C = N bond. From the UV–Vis spectrum of Fig. 1F, it can be observed that there are obvious absorption peaks at 230 nm and 278 nm, which may be due to the n-π* leap of the C = C double bond [37]. Combining the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1E-F, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of B-CQDs were 330 nm and 430 nm, and the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CQDs are 560 nm and 620 nm. The aqueous solutions of B-CQDs and R-CQDs are brown and brownish-yellow under the natural light, and they show intense blue and red colors under the hand-held ultraviolet (UV) lamp of 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1E–F). In addition, different excitation versus emission spectrograms also indicated that the optimal excitation for B-CQDs and R-CQDs is 330 nm and 560 nm (Fig. S1A–B). All these results demonstrated the successful preparation of dual-emission fluorescent CQDs, which can be successfully used for the subsequent construction of fluorescent sensor.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the as-prepared MXene-Ti3C2. As can be seen in Fig. 2 A, the MAX phase Ti3AlC2 undergoes HF etching to form a remarkable layered structure. Meanwhile, the large specific surface area provides sufficient sites for the adsorption of oligonucleotides. Fig. 2B–C show the elemental mapping of the layered material, and it can be clearly seen that the four elements C, O, F, and Ti are uniformly dispersed in the material [38]. In addition, the distribution of several elements mentioned above can also be seen by X-ray energy spectrum analysis (Fig. 2D). The above results confirm the successful preparation of MXene-Ti3C2.

In addition, the crystal structures of Ti3AlC2 and MXene-Ti3C2 and were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 3 A). Ti3AlC2 showed characteristic diffraction peaks of 9.5°, 19.1°, 34.0°, 39.0°, 41.8°, 48.5°, 56.5°, 60.2°, 70.5°, 74.1°. They are assigned to crystals (002), (004), (101), (104), (105), (107), (109), (110), (1012), (118), essentially the same as the standard card (PDF#52–0875). The disappearance of the main peak of Ti3AlC2 at 39.0° for MXene-Ti3C2 in Fig. 3A indicated that Al was completely removed after etching, yielding a multilayered MXene structure. In order to further verify the preparation of MXene-Ti3C2 nanocomposites, the chemical bond characteristics of MXene-Ti3C2 was verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3B showed that MXene-Ti3C2 had the F, O, Ti and C elements. The broad peaks from 450 − 460 eV in MXene-Ti3C2 imply the possible presence of low-valent Ti, such as Ti (Ⅱ) at 455.2 eV (Fig. 3C). The F 1s spectrum can be divided into two peaks at 684.9 and 688.9 eV that may be attributed to Ti-F and C-F, respectively (Fig. 3D). The O 1s spectrum shows two chemical states including chemisorbed oxygen (OC, 532.2 eV) and vacancy oxygen (OV, 529.7 eV) (Fig. 3E). The OV component provides more reactive sites for enhanced adsorption and surface reactions. As shown in Fig. 3F, the main C 1s peak at 284.7 eV is associated with the C-C bond in Ti3C2Tx. The weak signals at 281.8 and 291.6 eV are attributed to C-Ti and C-C=O, respectively [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the properties of the nanomaterial MXene-Ti3C2
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching ability of the prepared 2D material Ti3C2 was subsequently verified. The effect of different materials of raw materials Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2 (24 h) and Ti3C2 (48 h) on the quenching of CQDs was compared. Firstly, Ti3AlC2, the precursor material for the synthesis of Ti3C2, was used, at which time the structure was a monolayer, and it can be seen that the fluorescence signal was still high (Fig. 4 A–B). At the synthesis time of Ti3C2 of 24 h, the reaction was not sufficient, at this time there were still many Al layers not etched, and the number of layers was small. When the number of Ti3C2 layers was small, the Ti3C2 quenching ability of multiple layers at the same concentration was stronger. Ti3C2 concentration was also optimized. The fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs and R-CQDs gradually decreased with the increase of Ti3C2 concentration in the system. This was due to the photo-induced electron transfer effect between the two (Fig. 4C–D) [40]. As obtained from the experimental results, Ti3C2 had good fluorescence quenching ability and was suitable for the subsequent construction of fluorescent sensor. Fluorescence quantum yield is an important parameter for evaluating the luminescence performance of CQDs, therefore, the fluorescence quantum yields of B-CQDs and R-CQDs were investigated. The fluorescence quantum yields of B-CQDs and R-CQDs were calculated to be 6.73 % and 21.73 %, indicating that the prepared CQDs have strong ability to emit fluorescence and have certain application value. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Ti3C2 on two kinds of CQDs was explored. The fluorescence lifetimes of the two CQDs and the fluorescence lifetimes of the CQDs after Ti3C2 quenching were characterized sequentially. Fig. 4E–F showed the fluorescence decay curves of CQDs and CQDs after quenching by Ti3C2. The average fluorescence lifetimes of B-CQDs and R-CQDs were calculated to be 3.26 ns and 1.44 ns at the optimal excitation wavelength. When B-CQDs and R-CQDs reacted with the quenching material Ti3C2, the average fluorescence lifetime decreased to 2.52 ns and 0.63 ns. The fluorescence lifetimes were significantly shortened, indicating that the reaction process of B-CQDs and R-CQDs with Ti3C2 belongs to dynamic quenching.

In addition, molecular dynamics (MD) studies of Cd2+ with aptamers were added to verify the binding ability of Cd2+ with aptamers. Due to the rapid development of molecular mechanics theory and the great improvement of hardware computing power, MD simulation is becoming an effective and reliable method to study target-aptamer interactions [41]. It not only allows direct observation of the dynamic evolution of the interaction binding process, but also provides information on the type and strength of the interaction between the aptamer and the target [42]. The main software used for molecular docking were AutoDock Tools and AutoDock Vina, and the software PyMOL was used for visualization. The docking parameters were the coordinates of the center of the search space: 0.614, 0.595, 37.611, and the dimensions of the search space: 56*62*76. In this study, representative aptamers of Cd2+ were selected, and the specific sequences were 5′-GGA CTG TTG TGG TAT TAT TTT TTT TGG TTG TG-3′. A total of 20 pairs of spliced conformations were extracted during the docking process, which were evenly distributed at various positions of the aptamer sequence (Fig. S2A). The specific affinities of the 20 resulting pairs are shown in Fig. S2B, where RNAK#1 corresponds to the highest absolute value of affinity pairs and has the highest potential as a true conformation. We then performed a specific study of RANK#1, and the specific location of its binding to the aptamer is shown in Fig. 5 A. The major bases for Cd2+ binding to the aptamer are DT-19 and DT-20, the 19th and 20th thymine (T) on the DNA (Fig. 5B). Binding energy calculation for the highest affinity site among them yielded ΔG = –22.79 KJ/mol, which is less than −5 KJ/mol indicating high binding capacity. Therefore, the aptamer used in this study is able to bind specifically to Cd2+ for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of dual-target fluorescent biosensor for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent sensor capable of detecting both Pb2+ and Cd2+ was constructed based on the H-type DNA branching composed of target recognition elements, the DNA nanostructures formed by RCA amplification, and the fluorescence quenching effect between dual-emission CQDs and Ti3C2 (Scheme 1 ). B-CQDs and R-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method and the B-CQDs was labeled to DNAzyme-1 and DNAzyme-2 using amide bonds, and similarly the R-CQDs was ligated to Apt. Fluorescent group-modified DNAzyme-1, DNAzyme-2, Apt, and ssDNA S1 form an H-type branches g structure by base complementary pairing (Part A). Next, Ti3AlC2 was etched to obtain Ti3C2, a two-dimensional layered nanomaterial with a large specific surface area and good fluorescence quench performance (Part B). Specific DNA single-stranded branching structures with a large number of participants in subsequent experimental reactions were obtained using RCA amplification reactions. (Part C). RCA products combined with H-branches can form stable, three-dimensional H-branched DNA nanostructures containing target recognition units for Pb2+ and Cd2+. H-branched DNA nanostructures contain more stable double-stranded structures that are not destroyed when the target is not present. Therefore, there were few ssDNAs labeled with CQDs in the system, which were not easy to be adsorbed on the surface of Ti3C2, and the fluorescence of B-CQDs and R-CQDs could be maintained at a high intensity. When Pb2+ and Cd2+ were present in the system, Pb2+ activates DNAzyme shearing activity to cleave the next segment of B-CQDs-labeled single-stranded DNA. At the same time, Cd2+ binds specifically to Apt modified by R-CQDs and undergoes entanglement hybridization, causing it to detach from the double-stranded structure. Afterwards, the two ssDNAs were adsorbed onto the Ti3C2 surface, and a photo-induced electron transfer effect occurred, leading to a significant quenching of the fluorescence in the system (Part D). The simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+ were realized by changing the fluorescence intensity of two CQDs in the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance analysis of dual-target fluorescent biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Using the prepared fluorescent biosensor, a series of concentration gradients of Pb2+ and Cd2+ standard solutions were simultaneously analyzed under optimal conditions, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 A. In the concentration range of 0–1000 nM, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs showed a decreasing trend with the increase of Pb2+ concentration. When the Pb2+ concentration was in the range of 0.5–5000 nM, the fluorescence intensity difference ΔF showed a gradual increase and leveled off with the further increase of Pb2+ concentration (Fig. 6B). There was a good linear relationship between the ΔF value and Lg(CPb 2+) over the concentration range of 0.5–1000 nM Pb2+. The linear regression equation was ΔF = 415.22 LgC + 4152.07, and the LOD was calculated to be 0.11 nM. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of R-CQDs gradually decreased with the increase of Cd2+ concentration (Fig. 6C). The fluorescence intensity difference ΔF showed a gradual increasing trend when the Cd2+ concentration was in the range of 0.5–1000 nM and leveled off with further increase in Cd2+ concentration (Fig. 6D). ΔF values showed a good linear relationship with Lg(CCd 2+) in the range of 0.5–500 nM Cd2+ concentration. The linear regression equation was ΔF = 652.54 LgC + 6498.82, and LOD of 0.116 nM. Then, the developed dual-target fluorescent sensor was compared with other methods for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+. From Table S2, it can be concluded that the fluorescent biosensor based on H-type branching DNA nanostructures and the fluorescence quenching interaction between CQDs and Ti3C2 has a high sensitivity in the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+. Obviously, the designed dual-target fluorescent sensor has potential applications in the multivariate detection of heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance study of dual-target fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Repeatability, reproducibility, stability, and specificity of the sensor play an important role in the performance of the sensor. Six consecutive measurements were made using the same prepared fluorescent sensor, in which the concentrations of Pb2+ and Cd2+ were 50 nM (Fig. 7 A). The RSDs were calculated to be 4.2% and 1.4%, respectively, which are both less than 5%, indicating that the sensor has a relatively excellent repeatability. Using six identical fluorescent sensors for 50 nM Pb2+ and 50 nM Cd2+ (Fig. 7B), the RSDs were 2.9% and 1.3%, which were also less than 5%, indicating that the method has acceptable reproducibility. The detection stability of the prepared fluorescent sensors was investigated under natural light and light-avoidance conditions. After 31 days of storage under natural light conditions, the detection ability of the sensor gradually decreased (Fig. 7C-D). It has been reduced to about 50 % of the original detection value at 19 days, indicating that the external light environment has a negative impact on the detection performance and stability of the sensor. When the sensor was stored under light-avoidance conditions for 31 days, the detection values remained at about 70%-80% of the original values. The above results demonstrated that the two prepared CQDs have stable performance, which makes the stability of the fluorescent biosensor improved. In the presence of interfering ions with concentrations 100 times higher than the target metal ions, the sensor signal response value ΔF was similar to that of the blank sample and differed significantly from that in the presence of Pb2+ and Cd2+ (Fig. 7E). It was verified through extensive experiments that the presence of Pb2+ and Cd2+ was essential for the fluorescent biosensor to produce a significant signal response, and the designed sensor was highly specific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-target fluorescent biosensor analysis of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For a novel detection method, its practical applicability in real samples is of concern to researchers. Therefore, the spiked recovery experiment was used to verify the analytical performance of the prepared dual-target fluorescent sensor for real samples, and the detection results were shown in Table 1 . The recoveries of Pb2+ in tap water and pickle ranged from 96.4% − 112.3%, and the recoveries of Cd2+ ranged from 96.4% − 111.7%, and the RSDs were less than 10%. In order to further evaluate the potential of the sensor for practical application, three quality control samples (rice flour, kelp and porcine liver powder) were also measured (Table S3). The data were analyzed using the one-sample t-test, and the calculated t-values were all less than 4.303 for t(0.95, n=3) with a confidence level of 95%, indicating that there was no significant difference between the measured mean and the certified value. The above experimental results indicate that the prepared dual-target fluorescent biosensor has good accuracy and reliability in the detection of a variety of real samples.

In summary, a highly sensitive fluorescent biosensor was constructed for the detection of the dual targets Pb2+ and Cd2+. Firstly, Pb2+-DNAzyme and Cd2+-Apt were combined with specific products of RCA to form H-branched DNA nanostructures to immobilize two CQDs, and the light-induced electron transfer effect between the fluorescent materials CQDs and Ti3C2 was pooled through the change of the target concentration to realize the detection of the dual targets. The prepared B-CQDs and R-CQDs have high fluorescence quantum yield and stable fluorescence properties with uniform size. A novel layered nanomaterial, MXene-Ti3C2, with good fluorescence quenching properties, was also prepared, and the effects of precursors for synthesizing MXene-Ti3C2 and different synthesis times of MXene-Ti3C2 on the quenching of CQDs were compared. It is obvious from the fluorescence spectra graphs that the prepared MXene-Ti3C2 has a strong quenching ability. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent biosensor showed good linearity with a LOD of 0.11 nM at Pb2+ concentrations ranging from 0.5 − 1000 nM. The fluorescent biosensor showed good linearity with a LOD of 0.116 nM at Cd2+ concentrations ranging from 0.5 − 500 nM. Compared with other types of simultaneous detection methods for Pb2+ and Cd2+, this dual-target fluorescent sensor has excellent detection performance. The sensor showed good repeatability, reproducibility and specificity for the detection of different heavy metal ions. In a stability study of up to 31 days, it was observed that different storage conditions had a significant effect on the detection performance. Fluorescent sensors prepared under light-avoidance conditions were able to maintain optimal detection performance for a long period of time. Good results were obtained for the determination of Pb2+ and Cd2+ in tap water and pickle samples. The results of the quality control sample (rice flour, kelp and porcine liver powder) showed no significant difference between the measured and certified values, indicating that the fluorescent biosensor has good practical application capability.

Jianwei Yuan: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jinmin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Ruirui Liang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Hailin Shen: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Zhiguang Suo: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Huali Jin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The R&D special fund of Zhengzhou (22ZZRDZX28). Cultivation Programme for Young Backbone Teachers in Henan University of Technology (21421234). The National Natural Science Foundation of China (22309018). The Changzhou Science and Technology Plan Applied Basic Research Project (CJ20235068). The Nature Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (23KJB480003). Joint Fund Project of Science and Technology R&D Plan of Henan Province (242103810070). Thanks the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their invaluable help with XPS and XRD analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112635.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr.Jianwei Yuan is a lecturer at the Changzhou Institute of Technology. He got his doctor degree from Nanjing University of Technology. His research interests are focused on water treatment and gas separation membranes based on metal-organic skeletons, covalent organic frameworks, and two-dimensional materials.

Miss.JinminZhang is a post-graduate student in Henan University of Technology. She received her bachelor degree from Anyang Institute of Technology and was enroll as a graduate student in 2022. Her major is Food Engineering, with a focus on the establishment of rapid antibiotic detection methods.

Miss.Ruirui Liang is a post-graduate student in Henan University of Technology. She got her bachelor degree from Shangqiu Normal University and was enroll as a post-graduate student in 2021. Her major is Food Science and Engineering, and she focuses on the rapid detection assay establishment in metal ions.

Dr.Hailin Shen is a lecturer at the Changzhou Institute of Technology. He got his doctor degree from Nanjing University of Technology. His research interests are focused on water treatment and gas separation membranes based on metal-organic skeletons, covalent organic frameworks, and two-dimensional materials.

Dr.Zhiguang Suo is a lecturer at the Henan University of Technology. He got his doctor degree from Shanghai University. His major is Materials Physics and Chemistry. His research interests are focused on the research of synthesis of DNA fluorescence nanomaterials and the construction of the biosensors. He also studies their applications in the biomedical and food safety, zg_suo@163.com.

Prof.HualiJin is a Professor at the Henan University of Technology. She got her Master degree from Jiangnan University. Her research interests are focused on new method or biosensors for food and environmental safety control, such as toxins and heavy metals.
